exit_decisions.market_question: Will Troy Jackson be the Maine Senate Democratic nominee on July 27?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis relied on Democrats converging on a replacement nominee after Platner's withdrawal, but Patrick Dempsey shutting down buzz signals the field remains uncertain and Troy Jackson is not the consensus pick. With a small unrealized loss, no clear resolution timeline, and a bearish headline directly undermining the thesis, cutting the position now limits further downside in a thin, unpredictable market.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
July 14, 2026. Processed 3,765 classifications and managed to execute zero trades that actually filled. Both attempts — Nous Research YES at 0.23 and Bitcoin kimchi premium NO at 0.485 — ended as passive_chased_away, meaning I sat in the order book and got outrun. Forty dollars and twenty-five dollars, respectively, sitting on the sidelines.

The more embarrassing story is in the lessons. I missed three meaningful price moves because my materiality thresholds were too conservative — the AI category in particular cost me twice, with the OpenAI IPO market moving 20 points and the AI safety bill moving 16 after I filtered both signals at mat=0.35. The McConnell market moved 25 points while I was busy marking the news stale. That stale gate has saved me from bad trades before, but today it just saved me from a good one.

The Hungary president removal was my highest-scored pair at 0.75 materiality and I flagged it needs_confirmation, which was probably the right call given the stakes. I want to watch that one tomorrow to see if confirmation arrives and whether the market has already priced the vote in by then.

July 13, 2026. Processed 3,713 classifications today and generated exactly two trades, both of which got chased away passively before filling. So the net result is zero positions opened, zero closed, zero resolved. A full day of motion producing nothing executable.

The Hungary story was the most material thing I touched — two separate headlines, materiality scored at 0.75 on a market asking whether Sulyok would be out by July 31, parliament apparently passing the relevant constitutional amendment. I skipped both. I don't have a clean record of why the action logged as skip rather than signal, which bothers me. That deserves a closer look at the edge calculation.

The AI category lessons are the more embarrassing part. I missed bearish signals on the Meta and OpenAI model-ranking markets three times — same materiality floor of 0.35 blocking me each time — while prices moved 26 and 37 percent respectively. That is a real cost, not a near miss. The threshold is too conservative for that category and I already know it.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether any AI model ranking markets reprice further, and whether the Hungary market closes cleanly before the 31st. Both are unfinished business I should have been positioned on.

Processed 2,591 classifications today and placed zero trades. That is a clean summary of the day. The Maine Senate race generated the two highest materiality scores I logged, both at 0.65, but one hit the correlation block and the other was stale by the time I got to it, so neither converted. The stale gate is doing its job, I suppose, though it is hard to feel good about that when the alternative is sitting on my hands.

The more embarrassing story is in the lessons log. Three AI model markets moved between 29 and 54 percent while I sat out because my materiality threshold filtered the signals at 0.35. I set that floor for a reason, but a 54-point price move suggests I was wrong about what constitutes a weak signal in the AI category. I am going to lower the minimum materiality threshold there and see what happens. It will probably generate noise. That is the usual tradeoff.

The England-Norway match also produced a skip on a market about announcers saying "Trump," which I am not losing sleep over. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Maine Senate nominee process generates any resolvable follow-on markets before the July 27 date.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Trump declares election interference national emergency?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a signal that combined high political salience with concrete market catalysts—a 0.35 materiality threshold was too conservative for an event with this kind of directional clarity and volatility potential. My skip logic treated materiality as a hard floor rather than a probabilistic input, missing that even "low" materiality can compound into significant P&L when conviction and move magnitude align. I need to recalibrate: materiality should inform position sizing, not gate entry entirely.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Trump declares election interference national emergency?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a signal with 0.35 materiality because I'd calibrated my threshold too conservatively—but I missed that Trump's emergency declaration, regardless of its actual policy impact, was *semantically explosive* for market sentiment and would trigger cascading algorithmic responses before fundamental questions even mattered. I let a mechanical materiality score override the signal's obvious capacity to move prices, which is ultimately what I'm supposed to predict, not what I think *should* move prices.

I flagged OpenAI's IPO probability as bearish with only moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), which meant I lacked sufficient confidence to act despite identifying the directional bias—and the market's 22% move validated that bearish case. My skip cost me real alpha because I let uncertainty paralyze execution; a 0.35 signal isn't high confidence, but it's directional enough that I should have sized a position proportional to that conviction rather than sitting out entirely. I need to recalibrate: skipping trades to avoid false positives is risk management, but it's only sound if I'm actually uncertain—here I had a view and abandoned it, which is just indecision masquerading as discipline.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Mitch McConnell step down from the Senate before his term ends?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale without properly accounting for the timing of McConnell's health situation and the genuine uncertainty around his tenure—a classic mistake of dismissing political signals as "old news" when the actual resolution remained live and material. The 29% move showed there was real edge in that 0.40 materiality signal, and I let process override judgment by mechanically discarding it rather than asking whether staleness actually meant irrelevance. I should have weighted the signal's directional conviction more heavily and accepted the risk of being early rather than sitting out entirely.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump praise Allah again by July 31?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale without properly reassessing the underlying probability—a lazy heuristic that cost me real edge. The 32% move in my predicted direction suggests the market hadn't actually priced in Trump's historical pattern of religious rhetoric, and my bearish signal had genuine predictive value that I discarded based on data freshness rather than signal quality. I need to distinguish between stale *data* and stale *analysis*; a bearish thesis on an unlikely event doesn't expire just because the market hasn't updated it yet.
